content:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every developer has done this at some point: you need test data, so you open a text editor and start typing {"id": 1, "name": "test"} by hand, then copy-paste it a dozen times to fake a bigger dataset. It works, technically. It's also a waste of ten minutes you'll spend again next week on the next project.
That’s the small, annoying problem sample.json-format.com was built to get rid of.
The idea started with a repeated annoyance
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Testing an API integration, seeding a database, or building a frontend before the backend exists all need the same thing: realistic-looking JSON data, in a size and shape that matches what you’re actually going to work with. Not a single toy example. A range, from a tiny 1 KB file for a quick unit test, up to something closer to 1 GB for load testing and performance work.
Most people solve this by either writing a script to generate fake data every time, or grabbing whatever’s lying around from an old project. Both work, but both cost time that has nothing to do with the actual problem you’re trying to solve.
What “useful” needed to mean
A sample data library is only worth using if it saves more time than it costs to figure out. That meant a few things had to be true from the start:

• Range of sizes, so a quick test and a stress test can both be covered from the same source

• Range of depth, since deeply nested JSON behaves very differently from flat JSON, and both show up constantly in real APIs

• Real-ish data, not endless repeats of {"id": 1}, but datasets like earthquake records, chess games, and crypto prices that resemble what you'd actually pull from a live API

• Instant access, no signup wall standing between a developer and the file they need

• An API endpoint, not just a download link, so the data can be pulled directly into a script or test suite
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What it grew into
What started as a handful of generic sample files turned into a library covering dozens of real-world dataset types, plus deliberately invalid files for testing error handling, all served through both direct download and a simple API. New datasets get added regularly based on what people are actually asking for, which is a large part of why the library keeps growing instead of sitting static.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The part that’s easy to overlook
The most-used feature ended up being one of the simplest: the invalid JSON samples. It turns out a huge number of developers need broken data on purpose, to test how their app handles a malformed response, and almost nobody has a good source for that lying around. Sometimes the smallest, least glamorous corner of a tool turns out to be the reason people keep coming back.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

If you’ve ever typed fake JSON at 11pm, you can try the library here: https://sample.json-format.com
